old_claim.note: Itineraries & Route Guides provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Roman large-scale administrative maps were produced from land-surveying practice; surveyors divided land, drew maps, and compiled registers for local and central record offices.

source_shape.source_locator: Pages 212-216: describes early imperial surveyors, groma-based survey methods, maps (formae), registers, bronze copies, and local plus central record-office preservation.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source ties Roman large-scale administrative maps to surveyors and their field methods, maps, and registers.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: A surveyed administrative map is produced by surveying practice; text itineraries may inform route knowledge but are not the method that creates measured survey maps.

ontology_before: Surveyed Road Maps depended on text route guides, blurring list-based itineraries with measured survey cartography.
ontology_after: Surveyed Road & Administrative Maps depend on surveying as the production method, while route guides remain a separate written route-guide technology.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: surveyed_road_maps now depends on surveying and no longer directly depends on itineraries_route_guides.
would_reject_if: The node were narrowed to unsurveyed pictorial itineraries such as schematic travel diagrams.
A source showed the earliest scoped maps were copied from text itineraries without survey practice.
The surveyed_road_maps node were split into separate cadastral survey maps and road-network itinerary maps with different prerequisites.
short_reason: Surveyed maps are made from surveying practice, not from text route guides.
